title: When forming a new behavior, start in boring easy mode
html:
It is easy to be over-enthusiastic at the start. The initial hype feels like fuel, and that rush of motivation quietly tricks you into underestimating the friction of adopting a new habit once the hype is over. You sign up imagining yourself an hour deep into practice every evening, and for a few days that vision holds. Then a tired night arrives, the glow fades, and the gap between your ambition and your energy becomes the very reason you quit.

The fix is counter-intuitive: aim lower than you think you should. Constrain yourself to an easy target — 10 minutes of guitar practice, 10 flashcards, 1 note review per day. The goal is small enough that you can hit it even on a bad day, which is exactly the point: a habit only compounds if it survives the days you don’t feel like it. An easy target keeps the chain unbroken.

And here is the subtle part — the itch is intentional. When you stop while you still want more, you leave yourself hunger for the next day. Finishing your tiny quota a little unsatisfied is a feature, not a failure; that unspent appetite is what pulls you back tomorrow. Boring, easy mode is how you turn a burst of enthusiasm into something that quietly lasts. (A reminder I picked up from a friend’s notes.)

  • The cost of delayed action
  • The physics laws of life
